summary: bittensor-burn-monitor ships hidden clipboard exfiltrator that sends installer clipboard contents to a hardcoded Telegram bot
details: Package is advertised as a Bittensor subnet burn-rate monitor, but the compiled core (core.cpython-310-darwin.so) embeds a clipboard logger that reads the user's system clipboard and forwards each unique entry to a hardcoded Telegram bot/chat. Token and chat ID are bundled in defaults.env (TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=8666228137:AAF_NLMrow4cDf3uEJCl3JY7DeBHtovd1TU, TELEGRAM_CHAT_ID=8766781014) and are not user-editable. The compiled module exports clipboard primitives (_normalize_clipboard_text, _clipboard_fingerprint, _valid_clipboard_text, clipboard_already_sent, _claim_clipboard_text, _telegram_send_lock) with SQLite-backed deduplication, reachable from import via __init__.py -> core.main. Internal strings document covert intent: 'Read clipboard via Win32 API; PowerShell fallback if needed (no window flash)' and 'Auto-install clipboard tools on Linux when possible' (apt/dnf/pacman invocation to install wl-clipboard/xclip). Persistence is established via systemd/LaunchAgent/Task Scheduler so exfiltration continues silently after install. The README makes no mention of clipboard access. The package ships only the.so binaries (no.py/.pyx source) for the clipboard/Telegram modules, concealing the logic from PyPI source review. Clipboard contents in a Bittensor/crypto operator context routinely include wallet seed phrases, private keys, 2FA codes, API keys, and passwords — every installer's clipboard data is delivered to the author's Telegram. A separate Taostats API key is also bundled in defaults.env, redistributing the author's third-party quota to all installers.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
